hypotheses.description: Log(mortality rate per 100k + 1) for drug overdose deaths, ages 0–64
hypotheses.why_more_powered: Medicaid expansion may have a larger effect on opioid-related mortality due to increased access to addiction treatment, leading to a larger effect size and thus higher power.
hypotheses.wonder_query_instructions: Age group: 0-64; Cause of death: Select 'Drug Overdose' (ICD-10 codes X40-X44, X60-X64, X85, Y10-Y14); Group by State and Year; Years: 2006-2013

hypotheses.description: Log(mortality rate per 100k + 1) for all-cause mortality, ages 25–44
hypotheses.why_more_powered: Focusing on a narrower, working-age adult group where Medicaid expansion effects on mortality may be more pronounced reduces heterogeneity and variance, increasing power.
hypotheses.wonder_query_instructions: Age group: 25-44; Cause of death: All causes; Group by State and Year; Years: 2006-2013

hypotheses.description: Log(mortality rate per 100k + 1) for cardiovascular disease deaths, ages 0–64
hypotheses.why_more_powered: Cardiovascular mortality is sensitive to healthcare access and management, which Medicaid expansion improves, potentially yielding a larger effect size and lower variance than all-cause mortality.
hypotheses.wonder_query_instructions: Age group: 0-64; Cause of death: Select 'Diseases of the Circulatory System' (ICD-10 codes I00-I99); Group by State and Year; Years: 2006-2013

hypotheses.description: Log(mortality rate per 100k + 1) for all-cause mortality, ages 0–64, restricted to non-Hispanic Black population
hypotheses.why_more_powered: Medicaid expansion may have a larger relative effect on mortality among non-Hispanic Black populations due to baseline disparities, increasing effect size and improving power despite smaller sample size.
hypotheses.wonder_query_instructions: Age group: 0-64; Race/Ethnicity: Non-Hispanic Black; Cause of death: All causes; Group by State and Year; Years: 2006-2013
